title: Recursion of Thought: Divide and Conquer Reasoning with Language Models

structured.summary: The paper proposes 'Recursion of Thought' (RoT), a framework enabling language models to overcome context limitations by recursively creating multiple contexts for problem-solving. The method aims to improve reasoning capability across various tasks, showing promise in solving complex arithmetic and algorithmic problems.

strengths.point: Effective handling of context size limitations.
strengths.evidence: RoT allows models to solve problems requiring hundreds of thousands of tokens, overcoming previous limitations (Introduction, Sec. 1).
RoT can be applied to any sequence model, including small, randomly initialized ones (Sec. 3).
The source code is provided to reproduce experiments, supporting transparency (Conclusion).

weaknesses.point: Lack of novelty in conceptual approach.
weaknesses.evidence: The method repackages the existing divide-and-conquer paradigm without substantial new conceptual innovation (Introduction, Sec 1).
Insufficient details on how special tokens function seamlessly across models (Sec. 3).
Evaluations are restricted to arithmetic and algorithmic tasks, not covering real-world datasets (Methods, Sec 3).
The paper lacks detailed performance comparisons with other methods like CoT (Results, Abstract).
Claims about tiny models solving complex problems without pre-training lack detailed explanation (Results, Abstract).

weaknesses.point: Misleading claims about human-like reasoning abilities.
weaknesses.evidence: Parallels with human recursive thinking are suggested without structured evidence (Methods, Sec. 3).
Focus on arithmetic tasks without broader reasoning problems being tested (Methods, Sec 3).
Detailed descriptions on experimental settings and hyperparameters are inadequately provided (Results, Conclusion).
Bold claims on handling hundreds of thousands of tokens lack supporting scalability assessments (Results).

weaknesses.point: Unclear problem formulation for recursive division.
weaknesses.evidence: Lacks comprehensive method for algorithmic decomposition of complex problems (Sec. 3.3).
No complexity analysis or computational cost assessment of RoT's recursive framework is discussed (Methods).
No discussion on the practicality of implementing recursive functionality across models (Methods).

questions.question: How do the 'special tokens' orchestrate recursion across different models?
questions.motivation: Understanding their cross-model functionality could highlight adaptability and limitations.

questions.question: Why were real-world datasets not included in the evaluations?
questions.motivation: Validation with real-world data would demonstrate the framework's broader applicability.

questions.question: How does RoT compare in performance with recent advanced CoT models on similar tasks?
questions.motivation: Determine actual improvements in solving complex tasks compared to existing methods.

critique_points.point_001: Lack of novelty in conceptual approach.. The method repackages the existing divide-and-conquer paradigm without substantial new conceptual innovation (Introduction, Sec 1)
critique_points.point_002: Ambiguous methodology description.. Insufficient details on how special tokens function seamlessly across models (Sec. 3)
critique_points.point_003: Limited evaluation scope.. Evaluations are restricted to arithmetic and algorithmic tasks, not covering real-world datasets (Methods, Sec 3)
critique_points.point_004: Insufficient baseline comparison.. The paper lacks detailed performance comparisons with other methods like CoT (Results, Abstract)
critique_points.point_005: Unclear explanation of 'tiny models' performance.. Claims about tiny models solving complex problems without pre-training lack detailed explanation (Results, Abstract)
critique_points.point_006: Misleading claims about human-like reasoning abilities.. Parallels with human recursive thinking are suggested without structured evidence (Methods, Sec. 3)
critique_points.point_007: Limited problem complexity addressed.. Focus on arithmetic tasks without broader reasoning problems being tested (Methods, Sec 3)
critique_points.point_008: Questionable reproducibility claims.. Detailed descriptions on experimental settings and hyperparameters are inadequately provided (Results, Conclusion)
critique_points.point_009: Overstated impact on context size.. Bold claims on handling hundreds of thousands of tokens lack supporting scalability assessments (Results)
critique_points.point_010: Unclear problem formulation for recursive division.. Lacks comprehensive method for algorithmic decomposition of complex problems (Sec. 3.3)
critique_points.point_011: Lack of theoretical analysis.. No complexity analysis or computational cost assessment of RoT's recursive framework is discussed (Methods)
critique_points.point_012: Implementation feasibility.. No discussion on the practicality of implementing recursive functionality across models (Methods)
